text.title: django-quality-security Failure Modes
text.summary: Five antipatterns with concrete symptom, root cause, and fix. Each maps to a rule from `01-core-rules.xml`.

antipattern.description: Setting `SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS=31536000` immediately on a new domain or after first enabling HTTPS.
antipattern.symptom: Users cannot reach the site over HTTP for up to 1 year if HTTPS misconfigures; browsers refuse to fetch over HTTP regardless of server config.
antipattern.root-cause: HSTS is a one-way ratchet — browsers cache the directive for the full max-age and refuse downgrades. Reverting requires waiting out the TTL or asking the user to clear HSTS cache (effectively impossible at scale).
antipattern.fix: Ramp: deploy with HSTS=300 (5 min), wait one deploy cycle, raise to 3600, wait, then 31536000. Add to preload list only after running 31536000 for at least 2 weeks.

antipattern.description: Applying both `@ratelimit(...)` (django-ratelimit) and DRF `throttle_classes` to the same view.
antipattern.symptom: Users report blocked at unexpected request counts; on-call sees the limit fire at neither the configured ratelimit nor the throttle value individually.
antipattern.root-cause: Each library counts independently. A 5/min ratelimit + 10/min throttle limits at the smaller (5) but logs as the other; users hitting 4 ratelimit requests then 9 throttle requests see one limit at a time, halving observability.
antipattern.fix: Pick one per project. DRF projects: drop django-ratelimit, use DRF throttle classes only. Mixed apps: ratelimit for non-DRF views, throttles for DRF view classes — no overlap.

antipattern.description: Installing django-axes / django-ratelimit behind a load balancer without configuring X-Forwarded-For trust.
antipattern.symptom: One attacker triggers global lockout — every legitimate user hits the limit because they appear as the load balancer IP.
antipattern.root-cause: Default ipware-style IP detection treats `REMOTE_ADDR` as the client IP. Behind a proxy that is always the LB IP, making the per-IP counter collapse all users into one bucket.
antipattern.fix: Set `AXES_IPWARE_PROXY_COUNT` (or `AXES_IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER`) to skip the actual proxy hop count. For django-ratelimit configure `RATELIMIT_KEY` with `header:X-Forwarded-For` and trust only the rightmost LB. Test in staging behind the same proxy.

antipattern.description: Using CSP nonces while a caching layer (Cloudflare, Nginx proxy_cache) serves the same HTML to multiple users.
antipattern.symptom: Random CSP violations in browser console; inline scripts blocked sporadically; report-only logs show the same nonce across thousands of clients.
antipattern.root-cause: Nonces must be unique per response. Cached HTML reuses the same nonce, which violates CSP semantics — browsers reject scripts on every request that misses the original nonce generation.
antipattern.fix: Either disable cache for HTML responses that depend on nonces (`Cache-Control: no-store`) or switch to CSP hashes for static inline scripts. Long-lived nonces are not nonces.

antipattern.description: django-debug-toolbar middleware reachable in production due to a skipped DEBUG check.
antipattern.symptom: External scanners surface `/__debug__/` exposing SQL log, environment variables, template variable dumps.
antipattern.root-cause: Middleware list includes `debug_toolbar.middleware.DebugToolbarMiddleware` unconditionally; INTERNAL_IPS not restricted; DEBUG=True leaked into production.
antipattern.fix: Wrap middleware injection in `if DEBUG:` and confirm `INTERNAL_IPS = ["127.0.0.1"]` only. Run `python manage.py check --deploy` — it warns when DEBUG is True. Block deploy on any WARNING.
